ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for zucchini-and-egg dumpling filling. Mapped to closest parent class 'cooked egg and vegetable dish' (FOODON_03315595) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Egg-based dishes) used as the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for zucchini-egg dumpling filling in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this specific preparation.

preparation_method: boiled or steamed as dumpling filling

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of zucchini and egg filling is approximately 100 grams, equivalent to the filling used in roughly 6–8 medium-sized dumplings.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Zucchini, raw (FDC ID 169291) and Egg, whole, cooked, scrambled (FDC ID 172185), combined in estimated 60:40 ratio with sesame oil and salt seasoning
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for zucchini-and-egg dumpling filling as a composite dish. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: zucchini raw (FDC ID 169291) at approximately 60% by weight and scrambled egg (FDC ID 172185) at approximately 40% by weight, with added salt (320 mg sodium per 100g reflects salting step) and sesame oil contribution to fat. Input AI-generated values cross-checked against USDA component data and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated from USDA egg and zucchini component data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on cooked zucchini (GI ~15) and egg (GI negligible) composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for zucchini-and-egg dumpling filling. GI estimated at 25 based on: cooked zucchini GI approximately 15 (University of Sydney GI Database, analogous to summer squash) and whole egg GI negligible (protein-fat dominant, no meaningful glycemic response). Composite GI weighted toward zucchini carbohydrate fraction. Glycemic load per 100g serving = GI (25) × carbohydrate_g (2.5) / 100 = 0.625, rounded to 0.6. This is a very low glycemic load. When consumed as dumpling filling with wheat wrappers, overall meal GI and GL will be substantially higher.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 320 mg per 100g from salting step; potassium 220 mg per 100g provides partial counterbalance
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should be aware that the salting-and-squeezing step used to remove moisture from zucchini contributes approximately 320 mg sodium per 100g of filling; total sodium intake should be monitored carefully, particularly when dipping sauces are added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5g per 100g) and low glycemic index (~25); protein and fat from egg slow gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: The filling itself is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index; however, total meal glycemic load increases substantially when the filling is consumed inside wheat-flour dumpling wrappers, which should be accounted for in carbohydrate budgeting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate cholesterol from egg (~145 mg per 100g); low saturated fat (1.8g); sesame oil provides beneficial unsaturated fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The filling is low in saturated fat and contains beneficial unsaturated fats from sesame oil; egg-derived cholesterol is present at approximately 145 mg per 100g, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total dietary cholesterol and egg consumption in the context of overall dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Zucchini is non-inflammatory; eggs are generally neutral to beneficial; no known pro-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Zucchini and egg filling is considered a safe, light option for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as neither ingredient is associated with pro-inflammatory effects; sesame oil provides minor anti-inflammatory benefit from its unsaturated fatty acid profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Zucchini and Egg (西葫芦鸡蛋, Xīhúlú jīdàn) is a classic Chinese vegetarian dumpling filling made from finely shredded zucchini (summer squash) and scrambled egg. The zucchini is first salted and squeezed to remove excess moisture, preventing the dumpling wrappers from becoming soggy, then combined with soft scrambled egg pieces. The filling is seasoned simply with salt, sesame oil, and optionally white pepper and finely chopped green onion, preserving the natural mild sweetness and delicate texture of the zucchini. It is particularly popular during summer months across China when zucchini is in season. The dish is light, easily digestible, and low in calories, making it a preferred choice for those seeking a nutritious but non-heavy meal. Nutritionally, the filling provides approximately 95 kcal per 100g, with 6.5g protein from egg, 6.8g fat (predominantly unsaturated from sesame oil and egg yolk), and only 2.5g carbohydrate, giving it a very low glycemic index of approximately 25. It is a good source of vitamin A (85 mcg RAE), riboflavin (0.15 mg), vitamin B12 (0.3 mcg), and selenium (10 mcg). Sodium content is moderate at 320 mg per 100g due to the salting step. Clinically, the filling is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease; sodium monitoring is advised for hypertensive individuals. When consumed as part of boiled or steamed dumplings with wheat wrappers, the overall glycemic load and sodium content of the meal increase significantly.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for zucchini-and-egg dumpling filling as a composite dish. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for raw zucchini (FDC ID 169291) and scrambled egg (FDC ID 172185) in approximate proportions, cross-checked against AI-generated input values which were broadly consistent. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked zucchini and egg. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: detailed moisture content for this specific salted-and-squeezed zucchini-egg composite preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; moisture varies significantly depending on how thoroughly the zucchini is squeezed before mixing. 2. Saturated fat (1.8g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.5g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2g), trans_fat_g (0.0g), and cholesterol_mg (145.0mg) estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for scrambled egg and raw zucchini in approximate 40:60 ratio. 3. Sodium of 320 mg per 100g reflects the salting step applied to zucchini before squeezing; actual sodium will vary by recipe. 4. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this filling exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. When consumed as part of boiled or steamed dumplings with wheat-flour wrappers, total meal energy, carbohydrate, GI, and sodium will be substantially higher than the filling values reported here. 6. This is a Chinese dish, not a Singaporean dish; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and have been left null. 7. The input source (Beijing Academy of Food Sciences) is not on the approved source list; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central as the approved source.
